It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. As I'm starting to run short of classic books to recommend, I'll start things out with a quote instead: 

"Be thou therefore prompt and active as the Sylphs, but avoid frivolity and caprice. Be energetic and strong as the Salamanders but avoid irritability and ferocity. Be flexible and attentive to images as the Undines, but avoid idleness and changeability. Be laborious and patient as the Gnomes but avoid grossness and avarice. So shalt thou gradually develop the powers of thy soul, and fit thyself to command the spirits of the elements." 

That's from the Theoricus ritual of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, in case you were wondering. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

replies.title: Re: What is going on?

We are moving into a period of collective crisis and transformation. Astrologically, we're just over six months from a Grand Mutation -- the shift of the conjunctions of Jupiter and Saturn into a new element, the element of Air. (The last of those was in 1842; the next will be in 2219.) Grand Mutations always mark major changes in society and history...and a lot of things going on in the world right now make it clear that yes, that's what we're in for.

The fact that people are telling you that they think your life choices are better than theirs is an excellent sign, though. comments.title: Astral Thrift Store?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

My 54-year-old brother died of a heart attack last Monday night. He had many challenges in life, including mild mental retardation and mental health issues. Visiting thrift stores was his life-long favorite thing to do.

Just before dawn three days after his death, while dozing in bed, I distinctly heard a light switch in my head flick on and off, and sensed a very faint, quick glow. Then, I flipped over on my other side and heard wind chimes. They were lovely. And finally, I heard something drop within what sounded like a small animal cage; a springy, metallic sound.

Totally weird, pretty funny, really, and sounded like I’d imagine my brother trolling the miscellaneous section of the thrift store.

May I ask your take on this?

Thanks,
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Astral Thrift Store?

Second, it's quite possible that he was trying to communicate with you. The dead have varying levels of consciousness; some are wide awake and able to talk clearly, some are in what resembles a dream state and can communicate only in symbols. My immediate guess is that this was his way of saying, "I'm fine, I'm happy, please don't worry about me."

comments.title: Talismans
comments.content:
Hi, JMG, I just finished reading a Voyage to Hyperborea. I thoroughly enjoyed all of it, but I laughed delightedly when I read the line: "Welcome to Collinsport...", having spend much time there in my early youth, including with the "strange old family that founded it..."

Throughout the books set in the Haliverse, there is the running theme of setting to the work that is asked no matter how strange the tasks or the others working beside you. This also seems to be a theme running through old stories that are full of magic. I have to say as I've followed first Learning Ritual Magic and then Circles of Power I have sometimes felt that I am following that process.

Coming to my question: In Circles of Power I am currently working on gathering and consecrating my tools and looking ahead at the chapter on talismans. I realize all my life I have probably unconsciously made talismans in some way out of many objects that I have owned over the years. Some of these have been given to others semi-consciously as giving "a part of myself", some lost to time, and others I still have. Can the deconsecration process that is given be used to deconsecrate objects that may have been unconsciously or semi-consciously been made into talismans?

BoulderLovin Cat

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content:
Excellent! One of the delights of writing the Haliverse novels was the number of things like that I was able to put in there; I took the time to visit some websites on Collinsport et al., found out that some of the original episodes had featured a trip into the future in which it turned out that the Collinswood mansion was ruined, and took that into account in my portrayal.

Yes, those themes are deliberate, and they have a lot to do with the actual practice of learning magic. I wanted to make the sorcery in the novels believable, and modeling a lot of it on actual occultism was a good way to do that! In my current fiction project, still in its early stages, I want to go further and have all the magic (and there will be a lot of it) be completely realistic, based on things I've done or seen done.

As for your question, yes, you can do that, and it can be wise to do so.

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content: Very much looking forward to your new project (not the OP)!

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content: Thank you! It'll be a while before it's ready for publication -- it's going to be a trilogy, three hefty volumes, and I'm working with a somewhat richer and more elaborate writing style which needs development and practice. Still, it's new ground, and that's always fascinating to explore.

(Her name is Cordelia Pauline Kitzberger -- she goes by Dee -- and she starts the story as a decidedly homely 28-year-old legal secretary whose sole friend is a strange old man who lives in the apartment next door...)

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content:
The last time this came up, I tried that for a few of my people and wasn’t too impressed. For example, Sister Nuisance got dinged for being petite, but her brother General was not likewise dinged for being short. HE got dinged for being a polyglot and for having a knack for the art of war. I expected the Reverend Fastleft to get dinged for his beauty, as did his mother, but he also got dinged for being the current custodian of the sulky sword and for getting hit by the thunderbolt. President Burnedout got dinged for being President of his country, for heaven’s sake. It’s a dirty job, but somebody has to do it. And so on. It all seemed so arbitrary. I think the test was drawn up in a moment of frustration by someone who’d read too many crappy books in a row.

To her credit, I notice she no longer likes her test and has come up with a new version; have you tried that one?

replies.title: Re: Talismans

Did any of your characters rate high on the Mary Sue scale, or was it just one here and there? As for the arbitrariness, it didn't seem arbitrary to me, so much as exhaustive -- but then I've seen a lot of Mary Sues and Marty Stus.

BTW, the only Mary Sue test I was able to find on springhole.net was the original one. Can you point me to the new one?

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content:
Nobody was actually tarred with the MS brush, not even the Reverends Mom and Fastleft. As I remember, everyone scored in the single digits. It was just that, as I said, it seemed arbitrary. E.g. docking points for being short. (I think characters also got docked for being too tall—sorry, basketball 🏀 players!)

And here it is, the new, improved, 15-question Mary Sue Test! Enjoy!

https://springhole.net/writing/does-my-character-idea-work.htm

replies.title: Re: Talismans

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content:
Probably the most important question is: “Is anyone going to find this person and his problems interesting?” If they are, I don’t care what color his eyes are or whatever.

I had a little trouble with the (long!) eye color section. I wasn’t sure if I was supposed to answer the question based on their world or ours. So I tried it both ways. I remember you’d get dinged if each eye was a different color. (???). That does happen. I’ve met 8 or 9 people like that.

Trivia: David Bowie did not have one green (blue? I forget) eye and one brown eye. What he had was an eye injury that left one pupil permanently dilated, thus making that eye look dark at a normal distance from him. I didn’t know that till about a month ago!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes! Setting to the work that is asked. I was rereading Dreamlands and noticed that every time Miriam Akeley was asked "Do you understand?" Nodens said "Good!" Because what she didn't understand was how she'd be able to do the job. To use terminology from another author, she "showed willing." And that was ans is the key.

Another thing I noticed - a lot of urban fantasy (other than the "monster slayer, slayer beds a lot of monsters" variety) - takes place "on the seacoast of Bohemia." The Bordertown series was very heavy on artists and musicians, for instance. The Haliverse heroes are plain working folks, where they're not academics. Well, and one multimillionaire family.

The Grey Badger

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: One of the things that bores the bejesus out of me in modern fantasy is the way that it so obsessively fixates on cultural clichés like that -- you can't just have an ordinary character, no, it's got to be someone special. The angsty misunderstood artboy hanging out in the counterculture -- that in particular has been done to death for decades. (Brecken Kendall is in some ways my counterblast to that cliché.) Fantasy used to delight in taking ordinary people and plunging them into extraordinary situations, and that's still the kind of fantasy that interests me -- so it's the kind I write.

comments.title: Astrology, Energetic Practice
comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer,

1) One of the things that first convinced me to begin taking astrology seriously was that, at a time when I had no interest in astrology and knew nothing about it or about planetary correspondences, I scried several planets using electromagnetic frequencies associated with them, that were converted into human-audible sound. I was astounded and even a little spooked when what I perceived through this scrying turned out to match very well with the traditional occult teachings about those planets. Currently I'm learning a strictly traditional Medieval/Renaissance approach to astrology, and avoiding outer planets for now, but as a future research project I would like to do similar scrying experiments with Uranus and Neptune, perhaps even Pluto or other bodies, and compare my perceptions against what seems to be truly known of their energies, influences, and correspondences. What would be the very best, most reliable sources you can recommend to check with? Any additional advice you may have about undertaking such experiments would be welcome, as well.

2) Is there a significance to the planetary ruler of the day and hour in a) natal charts, and b) mundane ingress charts? If so, what is the significance?

3a) For Anonymous last week who asked, 'Are there characteristic astrological signatures associated with the charts of individuals who become more influential and famous after their deaths than when they were alive?': astrologer Chris Brennan has mentioned using Zodiacal Releasing, a time-lord technique that determines which parts of a natal chart are activated over the course of a person's life, to track posthumous fame, and he says Vincent Van Gogh's natal chart is a good example for this application.
3b) Since the analogous concept of dashas is applied in mundane Jyotish, and the Persians developed a similar mundane firdaria system, couldn't such time-lord systems be a fruitful area of research as timing techniques in mundane charts? I'm looking forward to studying mundane and historical astrology!

4) Finally, an energetic practice data point: it has been my experience that both Wim Hof breathing and the Chinese qigong practice known as Shuai Shou Gong/Ping Shuai Gong or 'sway hands,' both stimulate the solar plexus center, much like the Western occultist practice for that purpose that you've described before on this journal. I'll leave it to others to verify this, but perhaps one should be cautious about mixing any of those, to avoid the risk of overstimulation and imbalance.

Thank you for your time and for hosting these!
Regards,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Astrology, Energetic Practice
replies.content:
1) I know of very little of any value about the magical dimensions of Uranus and Neptune. Magic tends to be more conservative than astrology, and so it may be that the work simply hasn't been done yet. You may just have to see what you get, so that other people in the future have something to compare their results to.

2) I've never encountered any attempt to use these. It might make an interesting project for experimentation!

3b) Of course! There's a vast amount of material waiting for intelligent study and experimentation, and that's part of it.

4) Thanks for this -- this is worth knowing.

replies.title: Re: Astral crud etc
replies.content:
We have the chance to watch, on a multicontinental scale, what happens when a Utopian cult implodes. The increasingly frantic hunting for scapegoats, the explosions of mindless violence, the debasement of language and thought into schizoid word salad and increasingly bizarre paralogic -- these are all standard features of the last stages of the cognitive dissonance that precedes the imminent failure of the dream. The cult we're talking about, of course, is the cult of social progress. "And great will be the fall of it."

1 and 2) Actually, collective madness is one of the ways that the lower astral can cleanse itself. We'll know that it's done some good if, in the weeks and months immediately ahead, more and more people shake themselves out of the trance and say, "What the **** was I thinking?"

3 and 4) That's very hard to predict in advance. My working guess -- and it's only a guess, so take it as that -- is that we'll see another five to six months of craziness, but it'll be fewer and fewer people being crazier and crazier -- and then it'll collapse into wet ashes, possibly very quickly. Still, we'll see.

In the 1840s, Mexico and the US fought a war for Texas. In Europe, famines and food shortages resulted in turmoil and rebellion, including the Irish potato famine, which as we all know caused many Irish to arrive on the shores of the US in search of a better life. In China, the Opium Wars were in full swing from 1840 - 1860. I don't think modern people have any idea of the gravity of the Opium Wars and why they were fought. Opiate addiction is and always was ruinous. This book of illustrations depicts the sorrow of one person's opium addiction in the 1840s with poignancy and precision:

https://www.gutenberg.org/files/58350/58350-h/58350-h.htm

The most clear parallel to our era may be the Great Disappointment of 1844 seems to be gearing up for a replay, except the disappointment in question will be over the failure of Progress instead of Jesus Christ not showing up on schedule.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That is to say, you want to change a deeply rooted element of your personality. That's a worthwhile goal, but it's not something you can do with a single, simple working! Your first job, which you've already taken in hand, is to understand the roots of the issue in childhood traumas -- that's crucial, of course.

Your second job is to build up your capacity to will, and to act on the basis of will. Regular magical practice will help with that, but you can also help it along with will-training practices. Here the basic rule is to start with little things that have no emotional loading at all. Once each day, decide to do something small and pointless, and then do it. Get up, walk to the other side of the room and touch the wall with your left thumb and pinkie only -- that kind of thing. Vary the pointless act, but keep doing it. That will get you used to using your will in small ways, and you can then work up to larger and more useful things.

Yes, magical workings can help this along, but they don't replace the core tools of self-knowledge and the development of will. Get those under way and you can start making a different life for yourself.

replies.title: Re: Mouni Sadhu
replies.content:
Sadhu's well worth reading and studying, and you can certainly do this while maintaining a Golden Dawn practice -- I certainly did. I don't know of any groups that work with his system as such, though it would be great to hear that there are such groups.

Yes, it's a system. It's one take on one of the major schools of French Christian occultism, strongly influenced by the writings of Eliphas Levi and Papus, and by the book by G.O. Mebes, which I wish somebody would translate into English someday. (That I know of, it's available in Russian and Polish.)

As for Ramana Maharshi, Mouni Sadhu studied with the Maharshi after leaving Europe at the end of the Second World War, and wrote his books on Western occultism after that. So he did in fact integrate the two worldviews. It's one of the impressive things about his work.

replies.title: Re: Mouni Sadhu
replies.content:
The G.O. Mebes 'Tarot Majors' is now in English (publ. Jan 2020) translated by a 'Charlotte Louise Cowell'; and followed by a second volume: 'Tarot Minors' (April 2020).
A preliminary glance at the first volume suggests that Sadhu's 'Tarot' may be largely a direct lift from the Mebes.
A possible advantage to the English translation of the Mebes 'Majors' is the effort by the translator(s) to relate the material to 'Meditations on the Tarot' by Valentin Tomberg. (The Tomberg is a pretty massive expansion on the Mebes).

Re groups: not entirely sure, but I understand the Et Custosi Tutelae and/or the 'Society of Guardians' might use this material. Others may have more accurate information.

replies.title: Re: Karma and Sexuality
replies.content:
Small correction : sizes back in the day were smaller, not bigger. (Your point still stands.). In 197-something, the pattern industry got together and standardized measurements, something the ready-to-wear industry should do. A size 10 from one company was a size 10 from all the others as well. The size scale is still in use today, and novice sewers are forever finding out that a size 10 in patterns would be a size 4 or 6 in RTW. This is not only because RTW sizes have no consistency, but also because the RTW industry, especially in the more expensive brands, uses “vanity sizing”; sizes have grown bigger over the years so a fat old Boomer can enjoy telling herself she’s “still a size 10,” whereas if vanity sizing weren’t used she’d be wearing a 16.

Helpful hint for new sewers: measure yourself, find your pattern size, then buy your pattern one size smaller. This is because, when you make adjustments, it’s a heck of a lot easier to widen out than it is to narrow down. Usually what happens with this method is if it’s a top, the shoulders will fit with little or no adjustment and the part below the armholes will need widened out. If it’s a bottom, the waist will usually fit and the hips will need widening. Draw the shades, grit your teeth, and make it bigger where it needs to be. Nobody will know and it’ll fit better than if you bought your actual size and tried to narrow it where it’s too big.

Anu above
Enlil below (son of Anu)
Nammu right
Ki left

I still need to find two for in front and in back.

I asked the fews and it said don't rush into anything.

My question is this: The names above refer to older gods, a rather primeval set, and I wondered if it would be better to go down a generation and start with Enlil above and go from there.

replies.title: Re: Sumerian Pantheon
replies.content: It's not necessary to have all your deities in the same generation, so that's not a problem. I notice, though, a shortage of goddesses; it's generally best to have three gods and three goddesses, if you can do that. Ningal the reed goddess, Ninhursag the earth goddess, and Ninlil the wind goddess, might be options, and of course so would Inanna.

replies.title: Re: Sumerian Pantheon
replies.content:
Thank you for your answer! If I can use the older gods, as you say, then I will go ahead with that. I do have the genders balanced as you can see below. However, the last axis will have to be male/female if I am to have 3 of each in all.

Above: An (later Anu), sky father, supreme god, ancestor of all the deities, source of heavenly power, one “who contains the entire universe” (from ancient Sumerian text)

Below: Enlil, son of An, who cleaved heaven (An) from earth (Ki) and with Ki carried away earth while An took the sky. I would put Ki below but you said to have the same gender so I thought Enlil would be a good fit for below. In the Sumerian myths, he is a friendly, fatherly god, and is king of the gods. He is also the god of wind, air, and storms.

Right: Nammu, primeval goddess sea, mother of the gods, prime mover, the one who had the idea of creating humans. (I have till now been using “Great Spirit, keeper of souls” for this position, and when we started reading the CosDoc, I recognized it as the Central Sun to which we return, so a prime mover seems right here.

Left: I thought Ki, earth goddess, but further research says she may also have been Ninhursag, one of the seven great Sumer deities, mother goddess of the mountains, a fertility goddess. It might be best to use Ki for spirit below.

Front: Nanshe, goddess of social justice, prophecy and divination
Back: Nanna, god of the moon and of wisdom. (Enheduanna, who is an incredible role model for women, was high priestess of Nanna.)

Not sure how I can get a total of 3 gods and 3 goddesses and have same genders on each axis, but thought these two could work together.

For the elements:

Air: I wanted Enlil here, who is the air god of wind and storms, but I'm using him for the opening, so will use Ninlil, his consort, an air goddess, “Lady of the open field,” “Lady of the Wind.”

Fire: I know Inanna is hugely important, but I'm not particularly drawn to her at this time in my life, so don't want her in the opening part, and it's pretty clear to me that Inanna, goddess of sexuality, passion, love and war, is fire.

Water: Enki, god of freshwater, male fertility and knowledge, god of wisdom, magic and incantations, and arts and crafts, creator and protector of humankind. Seems a good fit for the druidly notion of water.

Earth: Gula, goddess of healing. I like the idea of including healing in the SOP, so hope this works. However, I now have 3 goddesses and only one god for the elements, so maybe Ninurta, also god of healing as well as agriculture? Or does it not matter for the elements?

Spirit below: Should I use Ereshkigal, who seems a risky choice, being a chthonic goddess? If I use Ninhursag for the opening, maybe I can use Ki here?
Spirit above: Utu, sun god of justice, morality and truth.
Spirit within: Would you recommend that I stick with “Awen” or find a deity that would work here?

Ok, that was a lot of work and thank you for your patience in reading it. I welcome any feedback you may have, even if it sends me back to the drawing board.

Thanks,
Myriam

replies.title: Re: Sumerian Pantheon
replies.content: It's helpful to have gods facing gods and goddesses facing goddesses in the four material elements Air, Water, Fire, and Earth, but it often works well to have a god facing a goddess on the vertical axis -- so An above and Ki below would work very well.  You can invoke a deity in the opening and then again in the six directions, too -- repeating isn't a problem.

comments.title: Evil gods
comments.content: Is there such a thing as an 'evil god', as opposed to one which simply does not align with our own interests? Unless I have misunderstood, when humans and other intelligent species mature spiritually, we progress to the realm of gods, and as such, should have achieved a certain level of compassion and benevolence, and limited motivation by greed, power, etc. While the punishments and demands meted out by gods may be harsh, they should not be 'evil', in the Buddhist sense of deliberately causing suffering for suffering's sake. With this in mind, I think of gods which demand human sacrifice - did these gods likely have some greater plan in mind which required so much death (eg. keeping population to sustainable levels or execution of criminals) or did the populace make contact with powerful entities which were not gods at all? (You state in your book 'Monsters' that blood sacrifice draws demons like flies to... well, you know)

replies.title: Re: Evil gods
replies.content: The difficulty in answering this question is that we don't really have a good working definition for the word "god," other than "a being that humans worship." Do some human beings worship entities who can be reasonably described as evil?  Sure. Do those beings belong to the same category as those beings most of us would consider gods? I really doubt it. I think human beings are not always careful enough about what they worship, and sometimes end up paying reverence to beings who would better be called demons, or to mindless egregors that mirror back the worshipers' own imbalances onto them.

replies.title: Re: Evil gods
replies.content:
Would any of the current major world religions fall under that criteria in your opinion? Like say roman catholics with Yahweh or Islam with Allah?

Thanks,

Andrew

replies.title: Re: Evil gods
replies.content: The problem with monotheisms is that there's no good reason to think that everyone who says they worship, say, Christ is worshiping the same being. There are certainly monotheist religious groups who act as though they worship an evil being, or a midless egregor of the kind I mentioned above. "By their fruits ye shall know them" remains good advice.

replies.title: Re: Evil gods
replies.content: The Bible even brings this up in Matthew 7:21.  "Not everyone who says to Me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but he who does the will of My Father who is in heaven will enter."

replies.title: Re: Evil gods
replies.content: In addition, there's good evidence that's not the case! I grew up Christian, and when I wanted to get back into religion I spent a while trying Christianity, and the entity on the other end with Anglicans, Catholics, and Eastern Orthodox folks worship are clearly different, to my mind.

My best subjects in school were Literature and History while Chemistry and Physics were a struggle. Is it just me or does everyone have difficulty with the very abstract images at the beginning?

I’ve been doing the Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram, which reliably seems to keep sleep paralysis beasties away. Would switching to the Sphere of Protection have the added benefit of balancing my unbalanced elements? What other practices would you recommend for someone with inadequate fire and earth energy?

3. In the post you discussed the Planetary Spirit of Earth and the attendant dangers of working with it. What are some good ways of working with this facet of existence?

replies.title: Re: Elements, Earth-Pull, and the Cosmic Doctrine
replies.content:
1) Everyone has trouble with the extreme abstraction of the early chapters. That's deliberate; it gives your mind a workout, and it also chases away those who aren't willing to put in the necessary work.

2) The usual next step is to proceed through a series of elemental workings, using more complex rituals such as the elemental rituals of the pentagram. You do all five elements beginning with earth -- the order varies above that a little between systems -- and ending with spirit. My book Circles of Power and Regardie's Ceremonial Magic are good sources for that work.

3) You're very likely the kind of person for whom careful doses of earth energy would be very helpful. I found the rituals and practices of Druidry very useful in this regard, though of course your mileage may vary.

comments.title: Geomancy question about employment

I did a geomantic shield chart the other day about whether it's in my best interest to pursue a potential employment opportunity for the coming year. I have a feeling they would likely want me if I offer my services (though there is some uncertainty about what it might look like, since it's a religious community and restrictions on their activities are still in place at the time being), so it's more a question of whether I am sure enough I want it that I should reach out to them, and whether it would feel good to be doing that job for the next year. Here is the spread I got:

First Mother: Colled
Second Mother: Tristwch
Third Mother: Bendith Fach
Fourth Mother: Via

First Daughter: Merch
Second Daughter: Bendith Fawr
Third Daughter: Carchar
Fourth Daughter: Elw

First Niece: Merch
Second Niece: Bendith Fawr

Third Niece: Colled
Fourth Niece: Bendith Fach

Right Witness: Llawenydd
Left Witness: Coch

Judge: Bendith Fach

My take as a beginner is that this seems like a fairly complex and nuanced reading. Bendith Fach as the judge is positive, as is Llawenydd as the Right Witness (which suggests I would enjoy the job?). I'm less certain of what Coch as the Left Witness means. That I should be careful not to be rash in my decision making? Or that there will be elements of the job that provoke me to anger (probably!), or others to anger?

I would very much welcome and value your thoughts and input, even and especially if you see the reading in an entirely different way!
Many thanks for your graciousness in helping with these types of questions, it's of great benefit to those of us in the early phases of the CGD who are working hard to get and understanding of geomancy to take root.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy question about employment
replies.content:
This is a nuanced reading, but yes, it's positive -- two favorable figures plus one unfavorable one is favorable, with some drawbacks. You'll find some aspects of the job irritating, but on the whole it will be worth taking.

As for the Way of Points, the First Mother deals with your own character and personality, and suggests to me that this job will help you lose something that you're ready to let go of.

"Eclipses that take place in water signs predict excessive rain, floods, and destruction by water. They can also warn of epidemics, and of serious unrest among the lower classes." Populist movement and agitation as predicted, but, while presumed to be MAGA, turned out to be something else, triggered by an unexpected event.

comments.title: American Artifacts / Psychic Questing / Terma Tradition
comments.content:
While looking at the website Ancientamerica.com, I saw this quote from Vine Deloria in a personal communication with author Ross Hamilton, and thought it would be fun to share...

***

Noted Native American author and professor of law emeritus, Vine Deloria, writes in a personal communication:

"It’s probably better that so few of the ruins and remains were tied in with the Smithsonian because they give good reason to believe the ending of the Indiana Jones movie—a great warehouse where the real secrets of earth history are buried."

***

A few weeks back I asked you, JMG, about your opinions on Andrew Collin's idea of "PSychic Questing". You mentioned you hadn't run across it before. Fair enough. This quote from Deloria brought it back to mind... as Psychic Questing is basically the hunt for magical treasure, or old texts etc.

Collins sums it up here: "If found in the Oxford English Dictionary, 'psychic questing' would be listed as 'using intuitively inspired thoughts and information for creative purposes, be it the exploration of history, the search for hidden artefacts or simply the quest for enlightenment'. It can begin with a strange dream, a visionary experience or an overwhelming compulsion which prompts the person to embark on a sequence of discovery. This often involves uncovering confirmatory facts, visiting sites and places revealed only by intuition and communicating with perceived external forces and influences through either meditational practices or magical processes. Often this takes the form of contact with a so-called genius loci, 'spirit of the place', or site guardian, which provides information in order that the quest might continue on to the next level. However, psychic questing does not have to involve outside exploration. It can simply revolve around archive research or just further magical or psychic experiments which all help the quester to gain a better idea of what they need to know.

In Tibetan Buddhism psychic questing is known as the Terma ('hidden treasure) tradition, and whole books have been written on the processes involved and past great achievements in this field. Those monks who become involved in Terma hunting are known as Tertons ('treasure finders'). However, similar methods of discovery have been used for thousands of years by enlightened individuals all over the world. There are many recorded instances of holy men or women being inspired to find hidden relics (e.g. Joan of Arc), occultists using necromancy to find buried treasure (e.g. John Dee and Edward Kelly) and psychics being brought in to uncover archaeological remains (e.g. Frederick Bligh-Bond)"

It sounds very intriguing. I like to explore and if I find something while exploring that would be a bonus, but I'm not sure about just setting out to find magical artifacts. Do you have any familiarity with this kind of thing (outside of the name "psychic questing")?

Thanks to you and all here on this net,

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: American Artifacts / Psychic Questing / Terma Tradition
replies.content:
I once drew up an outline for a roleplaying game that was to be called Terton -- the characters played Tibetan monks wandering through the Himalayas, doing battle with demons and monsters and seeking for lost Buddhist scriptures and artifacts. It would have been a lot of fun, but I ended up doing something else instead.

What Collins calls "psychic questing" is something that most occultists do at least a little of it. Half a dozen of my books were produced as a result of that process! So by all means have at it.

replies.title: Re: American Artifacts / Psychic Questing / Terma Tradition
replies.content:
I suppose I've done some of this in my reading & research... looking up stuff based on dreams and synchronicity and vision or intuition. So I'll continue...

...maybe there could be a Terton expansion pack for the WoH RPG!

comments.title: another unexpected visitor
comments.content:
Hello, Archdruid!

I hesitated to write this, because I feel rather a fool so doing. But reading of a previous poster's encounter with an unexpectedly helpful SAS man gave me the bit of courage I needed. (Thanks to them, thanks to you for this forum, and thank you _very much_ for allowing anonymous questioners. Also during this pandemic hysteria I've been trying to drop a few last pounds and build a better snatch form, so the previous poster should feel free to send the SAS gentleman my way.)

A few points of background that I believe are relevant (I promise I really have tried to be brief!):

I was raised by parents who were emotionally and occasionally physically abusive. Neither my sister nor I communicate with them any more, and that is (as you have sometimes pointed out) much the best possible choice. My father was the physical abuser, and while my mother was emotionally destructive, she did seem to me at the time to sometimes shield me from my father's wrath, for which I felt some kind of gratitude. Having been abused by my father I've done the usual and for most of my life avoided looking at the parts of me that look most like him. That's been changing over the last few years, and amongst other things I've come to realize how dwarfed and injured my own femininity is. I can't say I've done very much about it yet, but even the knowledge has been somewhat healing.

I was raised a Jew, but I must emphasize that this was a matter mostly of appearance-- there was no real spiritual life in my parents' home and although I was tasked, like many Jewish children, with learning enough ritual to perform in a bar mitzvah ceremony, it was clear to me even at 13 that the purpose of the ceremony was my parents' social aggrandizement. Nevertheless, as a young man living on my own I came to develop a much richer and more real relationship with the G-d of (some of) my ancestors, to the point of thrice-daily prayer in the traditional way and an evening a week teaching ritual to young children at the local synagogue. I quit when I realized that the major reason those kids were sent to my class was to perform in the same play I starred in. I haven't been very involved with Judaism for many years, since the birth of my children, and I now have many serious religious and other disagreements with the major Jewish denominations. I'm grateful to the G-d in whose covenant I was born for many things in my life, for many of which I prayed, and my prayers were answered. But I do not feel close to that G-d and do not feel any call from Him, at least not right now, not that I can hear. It is extremely important for me to note that I've always associated my Judaism with my father, as an inheritance, and like many powerful and life-shaping inheritances, there is an sense that comes with it of choicelessness, to put it awkwardly. I was born a Jew, and I will have a relationship with that G-d until I die, because of things that were done long before I was born. Not to be crude, but my own circumcision reminds me of that every day, which is, of course, exactly its purpose.

A last point of background: I have very little musical talent, nor much talent for visual art. There is ground truth in that (my sense of pitch, for example, really is not very good and I don't have great natural color vision) but I've been telling myself those things for most of my life, and certainly that is one reason I have done almost nothing to develop those capacities. A very sheltering circle, founded on a solid base of fear.

And here's where things get weird, in a good way. I think.

A few weeks ago, I was examining some material published for a well-known role playing game. I've played RPGs off-and-on for many years, but not recently. This material was well-written and introduced some interesting characters. One, in particular, was a deity, a goddess, that I'll call X. Within a few days, I began hearing from X. This was not something I invited, except to the extent that I was quite taken with the story-potential of the character and had spent some time imagining how I might use her as part of a world I've been designing for a few years (including imagining her communicating with denizens of that imaginary world). X speaks very clearly, but not dramatically, or at least not yet. The experience is lovely and sometimes almost ecstatic, but quiet and not disruptive. She is quite plain about her desire-- she wants to become more real than she is (a low bar, given how tenuous her existence would currently seem to be). Her "portfolio" as assigned by the game designers who invented her centers on self-expression. She's made clear what she wants of me-- she wants me to make art that tells other people about her. This idea of "proselytizing" is attractive and fearful to me. I want to tell other people about X, in the way that one wants to tell other people about a great discovery or a wonderful experience (and I realize how absurd that might sound, but as I say, just communing with her is really wonderful) and making art actually seems like one of the few ways that might be really possible without encouraging unfortunate reactions. I must emphasize here how unfrightening this experience has been. Startling, but not fearful. I'm not worried that I'm "losing my mind" or any such thing. Communication with her is delightful and I sometimes long to hear her, but I also have a lot of hesitation in my soul about treating with anything like a spirit in any way. This, I know quite clearly, is an inheritance from the religion of my birth (and much of my life). I've been working with that hesitation in various intellectual ways, including reading academic texts about the historical evolution of what Judaism has traditionally called "avodah zera", or "foreign worship". Hiding behind text is a pretty normal way for me to avoid commitment to action but it can't last very long, of course. It's something with which I have more wrestling to do.

It seems clear to me, really a little surprisingly clear, that I can understand what is happening in two ways which are both completely legitimate. X is something "outside my head", something native to another plane, that for reasons of her own, decided that I would be a good person to try communicating with, or she is a part of my own soul that is desperate for self-expression because of my particular history and choices. I do not find it surprising that a part of my suppressed inner life is finding outlet, or that a free being in an imaginal realm has decided to talk to me; these two views seem to me to be the sides of a coin. It seems to me that both are entirely true, although neither tells the whole story, which is not a story that can be told except through metaphor anyway. I've asked X about this and she agrees with me that settling on one of those stories is neither interesting nor important. I suppose it would be more important to a materialist. As a sometimes-pragmatist in the school of William James, I only want to ask what I should do, and it's very clear what X would like me to do, it's very clear to me that it would be healthy and good for me, and it's very clear to me that I want to do it.

Like many people who post here, I have answered many of my questions while writing this. I suppose these are the ones that are left: does anything about this matter strike you as worrisome or dangerous? I've emphasized the pleasure and exhilaration of it, but I have little basis for comparison. I can say that prayer to the Jewish G-d felt, at its most intense, somewhat similar, but the "flavor" was as different as might be expected. I literally don't know who I'm dealing with here, although I have taken some comfort in the fact that an unfriendly or dangerous power would seem to have many better choices of disguise than a truly obscure fictional character, and X's requests seem difficult to extract harm from. But as I say, I have little experience on which to draw. Is this what having a muse feels like?

Otherwise, do you have any advice for those of us (I know I am far from alone here) who are working through issues with, as the old phrase has it, "a jealous G-d"? Given my psychological background, I wonder how much of this is bound up with my understanding of that G-d as (in another vivid and old-fashioned phrase) "the G-d of my fathers", but then, there's no question that He really can be quite jealous, in the literal sense.

Well, that was far from as concise as I had hoped, but thank you again for providing this forum (I can't say how good it felt to "get this off my chest" anonymously in a forum where I can expect to be taken at least a little seriously) and for any advice you might have for my unexpected situation!

replies.title: Re: another unexpected visitor
replies.content:
These things happen. These days, especially, the line between the fictional imagination and the religious imagination can be very thin. Since I don't know what you're feeling, I can't tell you if that's what having a muse feels like; that said, plenty of people have had experiences like yours, and gone on to do fascinating things with their lives as a result. I know of no reason why you can't do the same.

I'm sorry to say I don't have any particular advice regarding the god of Israel; he and I have never interacted, and of course I don't have any personal involvement with the covenant your ancestors made with him. Here again, though, I know a lot of people who have left that faith and seem to be thriving.

replies.title: Re: another unexpected visitor
replies.content:
OP here; thank you! That is honest and useful, and I think you may have given me a really good clue to my situation by using the phrase "the religious imagination". That may be at the heart of this. It's been a long time since I heard any voice of organized religion take much notice of the imagination at all.

If you have time, may I ask why you say that the line between the two kinds of imagination is especially thin these days? Do you mean that much of what is currently taken for religion is fiction? :)

replies.title: Re: another unexpected visitor
replies.content: On the one hand, our culture has developed the fictional imagination to a much greater degree than most previous cultures; on the other, our religions leave little if any room for the free play of imagination, much less its use as a vehicle for mythic and symbolic consciousness. So a lot of what would express itself as myth in other cultures expresses itself as fiction in ours.

replies.title: Re: another unexpected visitor
replies.content: Hmm. Do you suppose our development of fiction to such a level is because we don't use the usual outlets for imagination?

replies.title: Re: another unexpected visitor
replies.content: Yes. Human beings can no more live without myths than we can walk without feet or see without eyes, so if our culture represses the normal, natural outlet of the mythic imagination, novels, movies, video games, etc. take its place.

comments.title: meditation
comments.content:
Hello, and thanks as always for this space! I just wanted to provide an update and look for any feedback anyone wants to provide.

I've been practicing meditation and affirmations now for about 9 months every weekday morning. This isn't the first time I've tried meditating, but it is the first time I've stuck with it for more than a few weeks, or had any clear idea of where I'm going with the practice, and I'm really pleased with my progress so far. I started out slow and have built up to 15 minute meditation sessions (usually about 5 min 4-count breathing and then 10 min quiet breathing). I'm still practicing breathwork, clearing my mind, and visualization; I haven't moved on to discursive meditation yet.

A few things I've been experiencing:

1) I almost always feel heat and tingling in my palms and the soles of my feet. This started right away when I first started meditating, and has been surprisingly consistent.

2) Occasionally I get a sense of pressure in the middle of my forehead. I think I remember you saying at one point that this is connected to the third eye (pinneal gland?) - is that right?

3) On several occasions, I have felt a very strange sense like my body is expanding somehow. Normally my sense of self and body centers pretty tightly around my core, and then I can feel my limbs extending outwards from that. However, on a few occasions during meditation, I've felt that my core has expanded outward, so that I feel like I am a larger bubble of energy, and I find it very hard to distinguish what is my body and what is the space around me. So far the largest this has gotten is about an oval centered on my upper body, extending about to my wrists when they are resting on my thighs. (Writing that now it doesn't sound very significant, but I've been impressed with how stark the contrast is to my normal sense of body.) I can hold this sensation for the rest of my meditation once it starts; however once I move it's immediately shattered. During this time the palms of my hands are burning up and I'm very aware of them. I almost feel as though I just have hands sprouting directly from my torso, strange as that sounds. This has actually been one of my favorite sensations so far with meditation, although I haven't been able to do it on purpose so far. Any comments or idea what's going on with this?

4) I've been having a surprisingly hard time keeping my alignment during meditations, and as a former gymnast this was very unexpected. It has been relatively easy enough for me to keep from moving during meditation, however, I will find myself listing to one side, or to the front or back, without realizing when or how it happened. When I notice this, should I correct to what feels straight, or should I maintain stillness? I'm still struggling with the balance between relaxation and tension, and believe this comes from over-relaxing.

5) On a similar note, I am able to relax most of my body, but am still struggling with one point along my spine (right shoulder blade) where I seem to hold an incredible amount of tension. I don't notice it at all during my normal life, but during meditation it becomes a fiery hot point, and I haven't been able to relax it yet. Any suggestions?

6) I am also struggling a lot with color visualizations and color breathing. This surprised me a lot, because I normally consider myself a very visual person, and get a lot of joy out of color and vibrancy in the world. However, visualizing just a color by itself is so far nearly impossible, and I have to instead visualize a scene or emotion to remind myself of the color. For example, if I want to visualize yellow, I think of my yellow curtains with the sunlight streaming in and my cat sunning herself on the wood floor. If I want to visualize navy, I think of the deep blue of evening storm clouds. Do you think this is a helpful way to practice color visualization? I assume like everything else it just takes time, and I have no problem inching my way forward.

Anyway, those are my thoughts so far. I would appreciate any perspective anyone has to add. I also want to thank you so much for providing us with the guides to discursive meditation last year, because they were what gave me the confidence to start this practice.

Thanks for all you do!
Cym

replies.title: Re: meditation
replies.content:
All this is very promising!

1 and 2) Both of these are common experiences, and have to do with sensing energy flows in and around the body. The point in the forehead relates to the pituitary gland, not the pineal gland, which is much further back.

3) That's a new one to me. I wonder if you're perceiving one aspect of your energy body.

4) Typically that's deep-seated tensions working their way out. Maintain stillness; don't correct your posture unless you have to.

5 and 6) It's fairly common to have such things come up. Keep at it and both the point of tension and the difficulty with color visualization will resolve.

replies.title: Re: meditation
replies.content: Thank you for the response!  I've really been enjoying this whole learning process, and I'm so grateful to you for inspiring me to do so and for giving concrete information for how to start.  I've only ever dabbled in these areas of knowledge before, but now I'm feeling pulled to dive deeper, and I attribute that in large part to inspiration from reading your work over the last few years.  My eternal thanks for that!!!

comments.content:
Hello Mr. Archdruid,

I work in healthcare, specifically with many hospice patients. I was curious if, from your understanding and study, it were possible for a "soul" to have left a physical body, no longer be present with the physical body, even while the physical body still remains alive (for example, near the end of life when there is still a heartbeat.)

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's complex, because the soul isn't a single thing. The etheric body or body of life force remains in the physical body as long as life continues; when the etheric body leaves the physical body, the technical term for the latter is "corpse." The astral body, however, which is the body of concrete consciousness, can leave the physical and etheric bodies behind for a while -- in fact, it usually does so at least briefly when you sleep. It can happen that the astral body and the higher aspects of the self can detach more or less permanently from the physical and etheric bodies, leaving the latter to lie there in a vegetative state, until death finally comes.

comments.content:
Not really a question, but an observation I found while thinking about the prospect of a nine-day week and planetary hours, with Uranus and Neptune included, and extending the Chaldean order of the planets appropriately:

Unlike with the 7-day week, the Chaldean cycle actually repeats itself after only 3 days, not 9 - if I start it on Sunday, it repeats Sunday, Saturday, Monday over and over in that order. The cause is mathematical - 24/7 is a fully-reduced fraction, but 24/9 isn't - you can divide a 3 out of it yielding 8/3.

I am therefore wondering if the means of dividing up the day would also have to change - not 24 hours, but some different number, one that is already in reduced form when placed over 9. Either 10 or 20 would work (as in most decimal-time proposals), as would 8 (3-modern-hour chunks) or 16 (90-minute hours).

Using a 16-hour day to tie in nicely with the geomantic figures, and starting the week on Sunday with the hour of the Sun, the nine-day week would then run Sunday, Thursday, Ymirday (Uranus), Monday, Friday, Tuesday, Saturday, Aegirday (Neptune), Wednesday. Again, I'm not sure if there's anything to this, but I suppose time will tell?

Something interesting in the positive direction did happen though. Although I am no longer sure it was a good idea and probably what I experienced at the bar was the blowback from it. It was another cloudy day in Seattle and the incantation came to my mind when I was walking through the temporary “Seattle People’s Department”. I then saw some candles on the name of the people that have been killed by police brutality being lit in a sort of altar. I took a new table white candle out, some flowers that were around and separated mine from the others. Then... I started chanting the Incantation under my breath and something, very lightly took over my tempo as if I was being guided. A few lines in and my etheric body started to rev up big time! About a quarter in the dog next to me jumped closer to me and did a circle around me with a smile on his face. It’s human was ashamed, but I was thrilled to see a confirmation of what I was doing. By the time I was done, a wind carrying apple smell came to me and the sun was out for a few moments, as well as a bunch of people around me with a curious face as if looking for something pleasant. Said a prayer on the name of peace, reconciliation and wisdom and went away. I have not the slightest idea of what I did, but it was pretty cool although I am starting to see it might have not been the greatest of ideas.

1) How would you evaluate the CHAZ-incantation situation? Probably not the best and I think I will need to take more baths too...

2) What was the correct way of maintaining the props used to wash myself? (cloth and container) I assume, if I do not have to throw it away that I should only use it for that purpose. How can I clean them so nothing lingers or emanates from them if that is necessary?

3) I got the materials for the series of candle magic workings for my family and my brother and wanted to clarify something. For my brother, in order to bring peace, love and reconciliation you suggested a blue candle and to pray to Venus. What would be more potent, to perform this using the numbers for Venus in charity and in the amount of days or to perform this working say, every Friday. If the former, for how many consecutive Fridays?

3') I was thinking of using this as a three part candle magic working. One honey jar for the whole family, the blue candle working for my brother for those specific days and then use a white candle indefinitely for him as a sort of constant support afterwards. Would this be a sounds way of doing it? I was thinking that since the first two have a limited time-frame that work is done I wouldn't see issues of dividing my will but I am not sure about it.

Thank you as always!
I don't know what would be of me in our current situation if it were not for you work and these Magic Mondays. My life is really starting to see some serious change because of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I'm not sure I would have done that, but if it had good results, it had good results. Regular banishings and/or hoodoo baths would be a good plan.

2) Yes, and you should wash them thoroughly between uses. Launder them if you can; if not, wash them good and clean in cold water.

3) Do it every Friday for seven consecutive Fridays, and then see what results you get. As for the multiple workings, that's fairly common in hoodoo, and it's not as serious an issue as in ceremonial magic.
